meta.title: BMW X2 20i Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW X2 20i engine — B48 2.0 turbo petrol. F39 and U10 generations, real UK costs and reliability. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X2 20i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned B48 engine typically costs £2,800–£4,800 supply only depending on generation, with the U10 version sitting at £3,200–£5,500. Fitting adds £900–£1,400. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X2 20i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B48 is rated ★★★★☆ — it's one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. Unlike the N20, the B48 has the timing chain at the front and uses a more robust design, eliminating the N20's chain guide failure. However, cooling system issues (water pump, thermostat) and occasional HPFP failures have been reported.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X2 20i?

acceptedAnswer.text: Water pump and cooling system failures, oil filter housing leaks, and occasional high-pressure fuel pump issues are the most common. None typically lead to full engine replacement if caught early — preventative cooling system maintenance is the priority.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW X2 20i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Against a £8,000–£15,000 X2 20i, a £3,800 recon engine is a manageable 25–47% of the vehicle's value — worth it for a well-maintained example. Given the X2's strong retained values, replacement is usually viable for vehicles in good condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X2 20i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The X2 20i uses the B48 2.0-litre turbo petrol engine across all generations. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the B48's water pump, or does it need a new engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the water pump is a straightforward repair costing £500–£1,200. The B48's water pump is the most common service item on this engine; replacing it as a preventative measure at 60,000 miles is strongly recommended.

Modern X2 20i — B48 with mild-hybrid on some; same reliable architecture with slightly higher output.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: BMW X2 20i — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Straightforward gasket replacement; catch it early
Specialist replacement; don't let misfires damage the cat
If caught early — far less common than N20 issues
If head gasket or block damage has occurred, replacement may be cheaper

buyingChecks.title: Buying This X2 20i? Check These First
buyIf: * Full BMW service history with documented oil-change intervals
* Water pump replaced at or before 60,000 miles as a preventative measure
* Evidence of regular maintenance — the B48 rewards care
* 2023+ U10 for the most modern iteration with mild-hybrid
avoidIf: * Service history shows repeated coolant top-ups with no diagnosis
* Oil leaks with no record of repair
* Long crank times or hesitation — potential HPFP issues
* Engine warning light present with no diagnosis documentation

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The X2 20i's B48 is a reliable, modern turbo petrol — a significant step up from the N20 it replaced. Cooling system maintenance is the key to longevity. Buy on service history and proof of preventative water pump replacement. If you're replacing, a reconditioned unit with a 12-month warranty is the sweet spot.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW X2 20i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle on startup (timing chain — far less common than N20)
* Service History — Including which engine era (B48 is the only 20i engine — verify this before purchase)
* Coolant Level — Is the coolant low with no visible leak? Water pump or thermostat housing risk
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil spots under the car or a burning oil smell (oil filter housing leak)
* Turbo Boost — Test acceleration; any hesitation, lag, or whistling?
* HPFP Health — Long crank times? Hesitation? HPFP issues are a known occasional failure
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved cooling or fuelling issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures; the B48 should pass easily if well-maintained
